section.title: Parables and the Calming of the Storm
section.sectionRationale: Mark gathers Jesus' kingdom parables into one great teaching day by the sea, the sower and its explanation, the lamp, the measure, the secretly growing seed, and the mustard seed, all unfolding the mystery of the kingdom. Then, at evening, He crosses the lake and stills a deadly storm with a word, so that His teaching about the hidden kingdom is sealed by a display of the Creator's own authority.
section.thesis: The kingdom of God comes hidden and small, like seed sown in varied soils and a mustard grain, growing by God's own power to a great harvest; and the King who teaches its mystery is the Lord of creation who rules wind and wave with a word, so the only fitting response is to hear, receive, and trust.
section.frameNote: Six beats: the parable of the sower; the purpose of parables; the sower explained; the lamp and the measure; the growing seed and the mustard seed; and the stilling of the storm.

units.label: The parable of the sower
units.note: a farmer went out to sow

verses.text: Once again Jesus began to teach beside the sea, and such a large crowd gathered around Him that He got into a boat and sat in it, while all the people crowded along the shore.
And He taught them many things in {{parabole|parables}}, and in His teaching He said,
“Listen! A farmer went out to sow his seed.
And as he was sowing, some seed fell along the path, and the birds came and devoured it.
Some fell on rocky ground, where it did not have much soil. It sprang up quickly because the soil was shallow.
But when the sun rose, the seedlings were scorched, and they withered because they had no root.
Other seed fell among thorns, which grew up and choked the seedlings, and they yielded no crop.
Still other seed fell on good soil, where it sprouted, grew up, and produced a crop, one bearing thirtyfold, another sixtyfold, and another a hundredfold.”
Then Jesus said, “He who has ears to hear, let him hear.”

units.label: The purpose of the parables
units.note: the mystery of the kingdom given to you

verses.text: As soon as Jesus was alone with the Twelve and those around Him, they asked Him about the parable.
He replied, “The {{mysterion|mystery}} of the kingdom of God has been given to you, but to those on the outside everything is expressed in parables,
so that, ‘they may be ever seeing but never perceiving, and ever hearing but never understanding; otherwise they might turn and be forgiven.’”
Then Jesus said to them, “Do you not understand this parable? Then how will you understand any of the parables?
The farmer sows the {{logos|word}}.
Some are like the seeds along the path, where the word is sown. As soon as they hear it, Satan comes and takes away the word that was sown in them.
Some are like the seeds sown on rocky ground. They hear the word and at once receive it with joy.
But they themselves have no root, and they remain for only a season. When trouble or persecution comes because of the word, they quickly fall away.
Others are like the seeds sown among the thorns. They hear the word,
but the worries of this life, the deceitfulness of wealth, and the desire for other things come in and choke the word, and it becomes unfruitful.
Still others are like the seeds sown on good soil. They hear the word, receive it, and produce a crop, thirtyfold, sixtyfold, or a hundredfold.”

units.label: The lamp and the measure
units.note: nothing hidden that will not be disclosed

verses.text: Jesus also said to them, “Does anyone bring in a lamp to put it under a basket or under a bed? Doesn’t he set it on a stand?
For there is nothing hidden that will not be disclosed, and nothing concealed that will not be brought to light.
If anyone has ears to hear, let him hear.”
He went on to say, “Pay attention to what you hear. With the measure you use, it will be measured to you, and even more will be added to you.
For whoever has will be given more. But whoever does not have, even what he has will be taken away from him.”

units.label: The growing seed and the mustard seed
units.note: the kingdom grows by God's own power

verses.text: Jesus also said, “The kingdom of God is like a man who scatters seed on the ground.
Night and day he sleeps and wakes, and the seed sprouts and grows, though he knows not how.
All by itself the earth produces a crop, first the stalk, then the head, then grain that ripens within.
And as soon as the grain is ripe, he swings the sickle, because the harvest has come.”
Then He asked, “To what can we compare the kingdom of God? With what parable shall we present it?
It is like a mustard seed, which is the smallest of all seeds sown upon the earth.
But after it is planted, it grows to be the largest of all garden plants and puts forth great branches, so that the birds of the air nest in its shade.”
With many such parables Jesus spoke the word to them, to the extent that they could understand.
He did not tell them anything without using a parable. But privately He explained everything to His own disciples.

units.label: The stilling of the storm
units.note: who is this, that even wind and sea obey Him

verses.text: When that evening came, He said to His disciples, “Let us cross to the other side.”
After they had dismissed the crowd, they took Jesus with them, since He was already in the boat. And there were other boats with Him.
Soon a violent windstorm came up, and the waves were breaking over the boat, so that it was being swamped.
But Jesus was in the stern, sleeping on the cushion. So they woke Him and said, “Teacher, don’t You care that we are perishing?”
Then Jesus got up and {{epitimao|rebuked}} the wind and the sea. “Silence!” He commanded. “Be still!” And the wind died down, and it was perfectly calm.
“Why are you so afraid?” He asked. “Do you still have no {{pistis|faith}}?”
Overwhelmed with fear, they asked one another, “Who is this, that even the wind and the sea obey Him?”

parabole.gloss: A parable, a comparison or story laid alongside a truth to reveal it. Jesus' parables both disclose the kingdom to those who hear with faith and veil it from the hard-hearted, so that the same story enlightens some and hardens others.

mysterion.gloss: A mystery, a secret once hidden and now revealed by God. The mystery of the kingdom is that God's reign arrives not in overwhelming glory but hidden and small, in the person of Jesus and the sowing of His word, to be grasped only by those to whom it is given.

logos.gloss: Word, message. In the parable the seed is the word, the gospel of the kingdom. Its fruitfulness depends not on the sower or the seed but on the soil, the heart that hears; the same word meets hardness, shallowness, distraction, and fruitful reception.

epitimao.gloss: To rebuke, censure, command with authority. Jesus rebukes the wind and sea with the same authoritative word He uses on demons, treating the raging chaos as something to be silenced. Only the Creator can command the sea; His rebuke reveals who He is.

pistis.gloss: Faith, trust, reliance. Jesus asks the terrified disciples why they still have no faith. Faith here is not the absence of danger but trust in the One who is present in the boat, the Lord of the storm, even when He seems asleep to our cries.

basic.context: Jesus teaches a crowd from a boat, telling the parable of the sower: seed falls on a path, on rocky ground, among thorns, and on good soil, with only the last bearing a great crop. Privately He explains that the seed is God's word and the soils are kinds of hearers. He adds sayings about a lamp not meant to be hidden, and about the kingdom growing secretly like scattered seed and spreading like a tiny mustard seed into a great plant. Then, that evening, a fierce storm nearly sinks the boat while Jesus sleeps. The terrified disciples wake Him, and He stills the wind and sea with a word, leaving them awestruck: who is this?
basic.hermeneutics: The parables reveal the nature of the kingdom: it comes hidden and small, spreads by God's power, and calls for a hearing that truly receives. The parable of the sower is a key to the rest, warning us to examine what kind of soil our hearts are. The storm scene is not a separate topic but the seal on the teaching: the One whose word is the kingdom's seed is the Lord whose word commands creation.
basic.originalLanguages: Jesus teaches in parabole, parables, that reveal and conceal. To the disciples is given the mysterion, the mystery, of the kingdom. The seed is the logos, the word. On the lake He epitimao, rebukes, the wind and sea as He would a demon, and asks the disciples why they have no pistis, faith.
basic.typology: The stilling of the storm echoes the God of the Psalms who rules the raging sea and stills its waves. Jesus doing what only the LORD can do reveals Him as God with us. The harvest imagery draws on the prophets' picture of God's final ingathering.
basic.christ: Jesus is the sower of the word, the teacher of the kingdom's mystery, and the Lord of creation who commands wind and wave. The disciples' question, who is this, is the question the whole Gospel answers: He is the Son of God.
basic.god: God's kingdom advances by God's own power, growing while the farmer sleeps. The same God who rules the sea in the Psalms now stands in the boat. The word God sows will bear fruit and reach a great harvest despite every resistance.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 6 lies behind the parable's purpose. Psalm 107 shows the LORD stilling the storm. Joel 3 and Revelation 14 picture the final harvest.
basic.oneStory: God's reign, long promised, arrives in Jesus not with instant overwhelming glory but hidden like a seed, sown in His word and growing by His power toward a sure harvest. And the King who plants that seed is the Creator Himself, who silences the chaos of the sea with a word, assuring His people that the One in the boat with them holds all things in His hands.

context.literary: Chapter 4 is Mark's first great block of teaching, a collection of seed and kingdom parables framed by the crowd on the shore and the private explanation to the disciples. The storm-stilling that closes the chapter begins a series of four mighty acts, over nature, demons, disease, and death, in chapters 4 and 5, all pressing the question of Jesus' identity.
context.historical: Farmers in Galilee broadcast seed by hand over unploughed ground, so some inevitably fell on paths, rocks, and thorny patches; the parable assumes this ordinary scene. The Sea of Galilee, ringed by hills, is notorious for sudden violent squalls that terrified even seasoned fishermen like several of the disciples.
context.canonical: The quotation in verse 12 is from Isaiah 6:9-10, God's word to a hardened people, showing Jesus' parables continue that pattern of revelation and judgment. The stilling of the storm deliberately recalls Psalm 107:28-29 and Psalm 89:9, where the LORD alone rules the sea, casting Jesus in the role of Israel's God.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To unfold the mystery of the kingdom, that it comes hidden and small yet grows irresistibly to a great harvest by God's power, to summon hearers to receive the word fruitfully, and to reveal Jesus as the Lord of creation whose identity His works and words together disclose.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The parables teach abiding truths about the kingdom and about hearing the word; they call every reader to examine the soil of their heart. The storm narrative describes a unique demonstration of Jesus' authority; it does not promise that following Jesus removes all storms, but it does assure believers that He is Lord over them.

debates.question: Do the parables reveal the kingdom or conceal it?

views.label: They do both, by the state of the hearer
views.case: Verses 11 and 12 say the mystery is given to insiders while outsiders see without perceiving; the same parable enlightens the receptive and hardens the resistant, as Isaiah 6 foretold.

views.label: They graciously invite while justly judging
views.case: Parables draw hearers in with everyday images and leave the willing to seek understanding, while the persistently unwilling are left in the blindness they have chosen; the veiling is judicial, not arbitrary cruelty.

debates.bearing: Both hold that response is decisive: he who has ears to hear must hear, and the repeated invitation shows the veiling is never a barrier to the humble seeker.

debates.question: What is the main point of the parable of the sower?

views.label: A warning to examine how we hear
views.case: The four soils press each hearer to ask what kind of ground their heart is, since the same word meets hardness, shallowness, and distraction as well as fruitful reception.

views.label: An assurance that the kingdom will bear fruit despite loss
views.case: Though much seed is lost, the good soil yields an astonishing thirty, sixty, and hundredfold; the parable reassures disciples that, against apparent failure and opposition, God's word will produce a great harvest.

debates.bearing: Both readings stand together: the parable warns us to be good soil and assures us that, despite every loss, the sown word will triumph in a bountiful harvest.

study.originalLanguages: Jesus teaches in parabole, parables, stories laid alongside the truth that both reveal and conceal the kingdom. To His disciples is given the mysterion, the once-hidden secret now unveiled, that God's reign comes small and hidden in the sowing of the logos, the word. The soils picture how that word is received. Then on the lake Jesus does what only the LORD can do: He epitimao, rebukes, the wind and sea with the same word He uses to silence demons, and challenges the disciples' lack of pistis, faith. Their awestruck question, who is this, is answered by the vocabulary itself: the sower of the kingdom's word is the Creator who commands the deep.

typology.type: The LORD who stills the raging sea
typology.fulfillment: Jesus commanding the wind and waves, doing what Psalm 107:29 and Psalm 89:9 ascribe to God alone, revealing Him as God with us.

typology.type: The seed sown and the harvest reaped
typology.fulfillment: The word of the kingdom and God's final ingathering, foreshadowed by the prophets, Joel 3:13 and Revelation 14:15.

typology.type: The mustard seed become a great tree
typology.fulfillment: The kingdom's small beginning and worldwide growth, echoing the great tree sheltering the nations, Ezekiel 17:23 and Daniel 4:12.

study.christ: Jesus is at once the teacher who unveils the mystery of the kingdom and the King the kingdom is about. He is the sower of the word, and He is the Lord of creation. When He rebukes the wind and sea and they instantly obey, He does what the Psalms say the LORD alone can do, and the disciples are left with the question that governs all of Mark: who is this? The answer, whispered to the reader in the first verse and confirmed at the cross, is that the One asleep in the storm is the Son of God, God with us in the very boat, holding chaos and death in His hands.
study.god: The passage displays God's way of reigning. His kingdom comes not by overwhelming spectacle but hidden and small, like a buried seed that grows by His own power while the farmer sleeps, until it fills the earth. His word, sown widely, will bear a great harvest despite every loss and resistance. And the God who in the Psalms rules the proud waves now stands bodily in the boat, so that His people learn their fear is misplaced when the Lord of the storm is with them. God is sovereign over both the growth of His kingdom and the raging of creation.

crossReferences.note: God's word to a hardened people, ever hearing but never understanding, which Jesus applies to the purpose of the parables.
The LORD stills the storm to a whisper and hushes the waves, the act Jesus performs on the lake.
Receive the implanted word and be doers of it, echoing the call to be good soil that bears fruit.
The harvest of the earth is ripe and reaped, the final ingathering the growing-seed parable anticipates.

study.oneStory: God's promised reign arrives in Jesus, but not as the world expected. It comes hidden and small, sown as a word in human hearts, growing secretly by God's power like a mustard seed that becomes a great tree, moving toward a certain and abundant harvest. And the King who plants that seed proves to be the Creator Himself, silencing the chaos of the sea with a word, just as the LORD does in the Psalms. The question the storm raises, who is this, is the question the whole Bible answers in Him: God has come to reign, and to bring His people through every storm to the harvest.

quiz.q: In the parable of the sower, what determines whether the seed bears fruit?

options.text: The condition of the soil, that is, the kind of heart that hears the word.
options.note: Jesus explains the soils as different hearers; fruitfulness depends on how the word is received, Mark 4:14-20.

options.text: The skill and effort of the farmer who sows.
options.note: The same seed is sown everywhere; the parable's focus is the soil, not the sower's skill.

options.text: The quality of the seed being different in each case.
options.note: The seed, the word, is the same; it is the soil that varies.

options.text: Pure chance, with no meaning to the outcomes.
options.note: The outcomes are deliberately explained by the state of each hearer's heart.

quiz.q: Why does Jesus teach the crowds in parables?

options.text: They reveal the mystery of the kingdom to those given to understand while leaving the hard-hearted seeing but not perceiving.
options.note: Mark 4:11-12 quotes Isaiah 6; parables both reveal and, in judgment, conceal.

options.text: Because stories are simply easier for crowds to remember.
options.note: Parables are memorable, but Jesus states a deeper purpose of revealing and concealing the kingdom.

options.text: To hide the truth entirely so no one could be saved.
options.note: They do veil the truth from the hard-hearted, but the repeated call to hear shows they also graciously invite.

options.text: Because He had no clear teaching to give plainly.
options.note: Jesus explained everything privately to His disciples; the parables were purposeful, not vague.

quiz.q: What do the growing seed and mustard seed parables teach about the kingdom?

options.text: It grows by God's own power from small, hidden beginnings to a great harvest and a vast reach.
options.note: Mark 4:26-32 shows the seed growing on its own and the mustard seed becoming the largest plant.

options.text: It grows steadily larger over time.
options.note: True in part, but it misses that the growth is God's own doing and starts from hidden smallness.

options.text: It depends mainly on human effort to expand.
options.note: The seed grows though the man sleeps and knows not how; the emphasis is God's power, not human effort.

options.text: It will always remain small and unnoticed.
options.note: The mustard seed becomes the largest plant; the kingdom's reach becomes great.

quiz.q: What does Jesus' stilling of the storm reveal about Him?

options.text: That He is the Lord of creation, doing what the Psalms say God alone can do in commanding the sea.
options.note: Wind and sea obey Him, as the LORD stills the storm in Psalm 107; hence the disciples' awe.

options.text: That He has power to protect His disciples from danger.
options.note: True, but the deeper revelation is His divine identity as the One creation obeys.

options.text: That He was a skilled sailor who knew the lake.
options.note: He did not manage the boat; He commanded the elements with a word, a divine act.

options.text: That storms are not really dangerous.
options.note: The storm truly threatened to sink them; the point is Jesus' authority over it.

quiz.q: Why does Jesus ask the disciples, do you still have no faith?

options.text: Because their terror showed they did not yet trust the One present with them in the boat.
options.note: Mark 4:40; faith would rest in Jesus' presence and power even amid the storm.

options.text: Because they had woken Him from sleep.
options.note: Waking Him was not the failure; the issue was their fear that He did not care and could not help.

options.text: Because they lacked the skill to save the boat.
options.note: The rebuke concerned trust in Him, not their seamanship.

options.text: Because faith would have stopped the storm by itself.
options.note: It was Jesus, not their faith, that stilled the storm; He calls them to trust Him.

questions: Jesus warns us to examine what kind of soil our hearts are. Which soil best describes how you have been hearing God's word lately, and why?
The worries of life, the deceit of wealth, and the desire for other things can choke the word. Which of these thorns most threatens fruitfulness in your life right now?
The kingdom grows by God's power even while the farmer sleeps. How does this free you from thinking that God's work depends on your striving?
The disciples asked whether Jesus cared that they were perishing. When have you been tempted to think God was asleep or indifferent to your storm?
The disciples asked, who is this? How would truly grasping that the Lord of wind and wave is with you change the way you face your fears this week?

translationNotes.note: The words about seeing but not perceiving quote Isaiah 6:9-10; they describe the judicial hardening of those who persistently refuse, not a denial of mercy to any who would turn.
He swings the sickle because the harvest has come echoes Joel 3:13, tying the parable to God's final ingathering.
Silence, be still translates a strong command, literally be muzzled; Jesus addresses the sea as He addresses demons, treating the chaos as something to be subdued by His word.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Mark 4:1-41. Footnote markers, line breaks, and poem markers were dropped when assembling verse text. The em dashes in verses 8 and 20 were converted to commas. No em or en dashes remain in this passage. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
